does: This paper introduces the Configurable Safety Reward Model (CSRM), a reward model designed to adapt to heterogeneous and dynamically changing safety requirements for large language models. The core contribution is a jointly optimized approach combining calibrated safety compliance with reward modeling, paired with configuration-targeted data augmentation that maintains instruction adherence while preserving relative severity relationships between safety violations. The model demonstrates state-of-the-art results on configurable safety benchmarks—94.6% F1 on CoSApien and 75.8% F1 on DynaBench—without requiring additional human annotation, and when deployed for downstream safety alignment, produces language models with substantially improved safety performance across diverse configurations.
connects: This work builds on the longstanding challenge of aligning LLMs with human values, extending beyond fixed safety classifiers and instruction-tuned models that struggle with distribution shift and novel safety specifications. It responds to limitations in existing reward modeling approaches that assume static safety criteria, incorporating insights from recent work on dynamic safety benchmarking and configuration-aware alignment. The paper connects to broader research on reward model calibration, data augmentation for robustness, and the tension between generalization and specificity in safety alignment—addressing why prior standalone safety classifiers and conventional instruction-tuning fail when safety requirements evolve or differ across deployment contexts.
enables: This approach opens pathways for safety-critical deployments where requirements shift across jurisdictions, user groups, or organizational policies without requiring retraining or human annotation cycles. It enables more flexible AI governance frameworks by allowing runtime or near-deployment configuration of safety properties while maintaining model calibration. Future work could extend CSRM to handle conflicting safety requirements, integrate with reinforcement learning from human feedback pipelines in configurable settings, or combine it with interpretability methods to understand how the model represents different safety dimensions. The framework also positions reward models as configurable tools rather than fixed classifiers, potentially transforming how organizations approach safety alignment for heterogeneous populations.
